text: The laparoscopic view shows a small uterus displaced anteriorly by a large central cervical fibroid, stretching the round ligament. The surgeon begins by dissecting the uterovascular fold and carefully pushing the bladder down to the level of the urethral meatus, taking care to avoid ureteral injury due to the lateral dilation of the cervix.

The surgeon opens the retroperitoneum on the left side, isolating the uterine artery and lateralizing the ureter. The uterine artery is dissected, and a ligating clip is placed over the anterior division of the internal iliac artery, traced above the obliterated umbilical artery. The ureter is lateralized away from the ureteric tunnel, isolating the vaginal region from the fibroid. Similar steps are performed on the opposite side, with the ureter isolated and the anterior division of the internal iliac artery dissected and clipped to prevent blood loss during the myomectomy.

The surgeon makes a horizontal incision over the anterior vaginal wall to access the fibroid, which is located towards the caudal region. The fibroid is visible along with blood spill. Using a myoma screw, the surgeon applies gentle traction and counter traction to lift the fibroid off the anterior vaginal wall and dissect it from its base adherent to the cervix. The base of the fibroid is clearly visible as it is separated from the cervix. After completing the dissection, the surgeon sutures the opened vaginal wall, with the cervix dilated by the fibroid visible in the center.

The surgeon uses quilt sutures to completely close the anterior vaginal wall. The fibroid is then removed with a disposable morcellator blade. The uterus is observed to regain its normal size post-removal.
